---
title: Long-term implicit memory for sequential auditory patterns in humans
date: 2020-01-01
bibtex: '@article{Bianco_2020, title={Long-term implicit memory for sequential auditory
  patterns in humans}, volume={9}, ISSN={2050-084X}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.7554/eLife.56073},
  DOI={10.7554/elife.56073}, journal={eLife}, publisher={eLife Sciences Publications,
  Ltd}, author={Bianco, Roberta and Harrison, P. M. C. and Hu, Mingyue and Bolger,

# generated from bibtex; do not edit manually
journal: eLife
link: https://doi.org/10.7554/elife.56073
abstract: Memory, on multiple timescales, is critical to our ability to discover the
  structure of our surroundings, and efficiently interact with the environment. We
  combined behavioural manipulation and modelling to investigate the dynamics of memory
  formation for rarely reoccurring acoustic patterns. In a series of experiments,
  participants detected the emergence of regularly repeating patterns within rapid
  tone-pip sequences. Unbeknownst to them, a few patterns reoccurred every ~3 min.
  All sequences consisted of the same 20 frequencies and were distinguishable only
  by the order of tone-pips. Despite this, reoccurring patterns were associated with
  a rapidly growing detection-time advantage over novel patterns. This effect was
  implicit, robust to interference, and persisted for 7 weeks. The results implicate
  an interplay between short (a few seconds) and long-term (over many minutes) integration
  in memory formation and demonstrate the remarkable sensitivity of the human auditory
  system to sporadically reoccurring structure within the acoustic environment.
